Starting with R

Søren L.F. Lageri

2026-03-03

1) Use R to figure out how many elements in the vector below are greater than 2.

# We first define the vector given
rooms <- c(1, 5, 2, 3, 1, NA, 3, 1, 3, 2, NA, 1, 8, 3, 1, 4, NA, 1, 3, 1, 2, 1, 7, 1, NA, 4, 3 ,1 ,7 ,2 ,1 ,NA ,1, 1, 3)

# Then we define a new vector, where we filter out the missing data (NA) by using !is.na(rooms).

vectors_greater_than_2 <- rooms[rooms >= 3 & !is.na(rooms)] # We write 3 to not include 2.

# We print it
vectors_greater_than_2
##  [1] 5 3 3 3 8 3 4 3 7 4 3 7 3
# We find the total length of the new vector to get a value for the amount of elements which were greater than 2.
length(rooms)
## [1] 35
length(vectors_greater_than_2)
## [1] 13

In the original vector there are 35 total elements.

There are 13 elements in the list of vectors which are greater than 2.

2) Which function tells you the type of data the ‘rooms’ vector above contains?

# We create a new list, which runs TRUE or FALSE statements.
c(
  Numeric   = is.numeric(rooms),
  Integer   = is.integer(rooms),
  Character = is.character(rooms),
  Logical   = is.logical(rooms)
)
##   Numeric   Integer Character   Logical 
##      TRUE     FALSE     FALSE     FALSE

Since we are dealing with a vector, which has numbers, it is logical to expect that the data is numerical.

However, this was solved before we heard about the summary function str(argument). Which is simpler and faster solution.

summary_rooms <- str(rooms)
##  num [1:35] 1 5 2 3 1 NA 3 1 3 2 ...

From the summary function we can see that the data is numeric, confirming the previous result.

3) What is the result of running the median() function on the above ‘rooms’ vector?

# We use median(Argument) function
median_rooms <- median(rooms)

# Here we include na.rm = TRUE to exclude missing data (NA)
median_rooms_without_NA<- median(rooms, na.rm = TRUE)

The median when the missing data (NA) is included is: NA

The calculation of the median also includes the missing data (NA), which results in it being included in the median calculations.

Therefore, when excluding this data from the median calculations, the median is: 2

4) Interviews

# We load the libraries
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.2.0
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(here)
## here() starts at C:/Users/sl200/OneDrive - Aarhus universitet/6. semester/Digitale arkiver og metoder/DAM - R
# Here we load the CSV-file and count the missing data (NA) as null.
# It finds the CSV-file in our data directory.
interviews <- read_csv(
  here("data/U10", "SAFI_clean.csv"), 
  na = "NULL")
## Rows: 131 Columns: 14
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr  (7): village, respondent_wall_type, memb_assoc, affect_conflicts, items...
## dbl  (6): key_ID, no_membrs, years_liv, rooms, liv_count, no_meals
## dttm (1): interview_date
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
